result:
# The Texture of Waiting

I want to write about waiting. Not the dramatic kind — not waiting for biopsy results or waiting to hear if someone survived the accident. The ordinary kind. The kind that fills the hours between events, the kind we hardly notice until we start noticing it everywhere.

I've been thinking about waiting rooms specifically. Not as metaphor, though they are almost irresistible as metaphor, but as actual physical spaces that someone had to design and that we have to inhabit. Someone chose those chairs. Someone looked at a catalog and said: these ones. Stackable plastic or upholstered in a fabric that resists staining and shows nothing, the color of compromise, somewhere between beige and grey, a color that in another context wouldn't even have a name but in waiting rooms becomes its own distinct hue, institutional and oddly specific.

The chairs are almost always slightly too close together. You end up performing a complex social negotiation with strangers, deciding how much of your body to keep strictly contained, whether you can cross your legs without encroaching, whether you can look at your phone without seeming rude, whether looking at your phone actually is rude and if so to whom and on what grounds. You are surrounded by people who are also waiting, which creates a strange solidarity that no one wants to acknowledge. The waiting room is a democracy of inconvenience.

What interests me is what waiting does to time. It doesn't simply slow time down, although people often say it does. It does something stranger. Waiting makes you aware of time as texture rather than flow. When you're busy, when you're doing things, time is more or less invisible. You surface from a project and four hours have passed. But in the waiting room you feel each minute as a kind of substance. Not heavy exactly. Granular, maybe. Like time has become something you could almost hold in your hand and let run between your fingers.

There's a specific quality to this that I think is underappreciated as a human experience. The phenomenologists — Husserl, Heidegger, the people who spent their lives trying to describe exactly what consciousness is doing when it does the various things it does — they talked a lot about time perception, about the way present experience always contains traces of the immediate past and anticipations of the immediate future. Every now, Husserl argued, has this retentional and protentional structure, a kind of temporal thickness. But in waiting the protentional structure starts to hollow out. You can't anticipate specifics. You know something will happen — your name will be called, the train will come, the office will open — but you don't know when, and because you don't know when, your anticipation loses its content. You're left anticipating anticipation itself. Waiting for the waiting to end.

This is not always unpleasant. I want to resist the easy move of treating waiting as pure privation, as time that is simply absent meaning. There is something available in waiting that isn't available elsewhere, and we occasionally stumble into it by accident.

I'm thinking of a specific afternoon when I was perhaps eleven or twelve years old, waiting for my mother in a parking lot outside a grocery store. This would have been in the 1990s, so there was no phone, nothing to read, no entertainment architecture of any kind. Just the parking lot, the late afternoon sun coming in at that particular autumn angle that makes ordinary things look briefly significant, the sound of carts being collected, the smell of exhaust and something faintly sweet I could never identify. I sat on a concrete barrier and just — existed for a while. I wasn't bored. I wasn't thinking particularly hard about anything. I was present in a way that felt effortless because there was nothing else to be and nothing else required.

I couldn't reproduce that now if I tried. The machinery of self-consciousness has become too elaborate. Even in enforced waiting I am narrating my waiting to myself, analyzing it, pulling out my phone, checking things that don't need to be checked, reading things I won't remember. The waiting that was available to me at eleven has closed off. I wonder sometimes if this is loss or just change, and I lean toward loss, but I hold that loosely.

---

There's a type of waiting that deserves its own category and that I think about separately: waiting on hold.

Waiting on hold is one of the genuinely novel experiences that modernity has produced. There is nothing like it in human history before perhaps the last century. You are frozen, unable to do much of anything meaningful, tethered to a phone, subjected to music that was designed not to be listened to, periodically interrupted by a recorded voice that thanks you for your patience in a tone that makes patience feel like a failing.

The hold music deserves attention. It exists in a peculiar aesthetic space. It can't be too engaging because then you might become annoyed when the representative interrupts it. It can't be too tedious or you will hang up. It has to be precisely and consistently negligible. This is an actual design goal: music that means nothing, that registers as a kind of auditory wallpaper, that occupies just enough of your attention to prevent you from losing your mind while accomplishing nothing beyond that. The people who make hold music are engineering a particular form of minor suffering.

And yet I've found myself, in long holds, starting to actually hear the music. When you've been on hold for thirty minutes, the loop becomes familiar. You start anticipating the transitions. That xylophone figure. The slightly too-enthusiastic trumpet. There's a perverse intimacy that develops. You know this music now, better than you know songs you've chosen to listen to, because you've absorbed it through sheer duration. The hold music has colonized a small part of your memory without asking.

The periodic interruption — "Your call is very important to us, please continue to hold" — is interesting in its specific inversion of meaning. The statement "your call is very important to us" is structurally identical to something someone might say to reassure you, but the effect is the opposite of reassurance. It highlights exactly the thing you're trying not to think about, which is that you are being made to wait by people who have decided your time is worth less than theirs. The corporate voice has learned to deploy the grammar of care without the content of care, and nowhere is this more concentrated than in the on-hold message.

---

I want to make a lateral move here and think about waiting that is chosen rather than imposed.

People wait in lines for things they want. They queue overnight outside stores. They sit for hours in the dark before concerts start. They arrive early to claim good spots. This is waiting as ritual, waiting as intensification, waiting as the creation of a container that makes the eventual experience feel earned and therefore more significant.

There's evidence that this works. The psychology of waiting suggests that we value experiences more when we've had to wait for them. The famous "IKEA effect" — we tend to overvalue things we've assembled ourselves — has a temporal cousin: we overvalue things we've waited for. The wait becomes part of the experience itself, a prologue that shapes everything after it.

I think about pilgrims. People who walk hundreds of miles to reach a shrine, sleeping rough, developing blisters, eating badly. The walk, which is entirely made of waiting — waiting to arrive — is inseparable from whatever happens when they get there. The theologians have a word for this kind of structured anticipation: it's related to what they call the via negativa, the way of negation, the idea that sometimes you approach meaning not by accumulating content but by stripping away, by moving through absence toward presence. Waiting understood this way is not nothing. It is preparation in the deepest sense, the making of a space.

I don't want to oversell this. Plenty of waiting is just waiting. The bus that is twelve minutes late on a cold morning is not a spiritual practice. The DMV is not a pilgrimage. But I think there's something in the deliberate embrace of waiting — the waiting that people choose, the waiting people protect — that suggests we haven't fully lost the sense that time-before is part of time-during.

---

Here's a thing I find strange: the way we wait for people we love is completely different from all other waiting, and I don't think it gets enough acknowledgment.

Waiting for a friend who is late to arrive at a restaurant. The texture of that waiting shifts depending on how many minutes have passed and depending on what the relationship contains. In the first five minutes, you're fine, you're checking your own punctuality, ordering water, looking at the menu you already know. By fifteen minutes something subtle has changed. You've read the menu all the way through twice including the sections you'd never order from, the pastas and the gluten-free modifications. You've looked around at the other diners and had brief involuntary narratives about who they are and what they're celebrating or grieving. By twenty-five minutes you've sent a text, casual, no pressure, and the waiting has acquired a slight edge that isn't quite worry and isn't quite irritation and doesn't have a good name.

And then they arrive. And almost immediately the waiting retroactively becomes nothing. It dissolves into the dinner. You might not even mention it. The waiting was real, it occupied real time, it had real texture, and it disappears completely once the thing waited-for arrives.

This is one of the stranger properties of waiting: its extreme reversibility. Unlike most experiences, which accumulate and leave sediment, waiting often evaporates when resolved. You were waiting for a letter and the letter came and the weeks of anticipation become nothing. In memory the letter simply arrives. The interval before it compresses toward zero.

Unless the waiting was very long, or very hard, in which case something different happens. The waiting doesn't disappear; it becomes context. It becomes part of the story. People say, "I waited three years for this," and the three years are not nothing, they're essential. The waiting has been converted into a kind of weight that the arrival carries. This seems to be a threshold phenomenon, where short waits vanish and long waits survive. But where the threshold is varies by person and by what's being waited for, and I don't think anyone has drawn that map.

---

I should say something about digital waiting because we have invented a new form of it.

The loading screen. The buffering circle. The progress bar. We have built systems so fast that they can make us wait in intervals of seconds or fractions of seconds, and we have discovered, to no one's particular credit, that these tiny waits are maddening in a way that longer waits of earlier eras somehow weren't. A page that takes four seconds to load produces visible, physiological irritation in people. This is not weakness or impatience. It is adaptation. We calibrated our patience to an environment that no longer exists.

There's a famous study suggesting that pages losing users at load times of a few seconds, the relationship between latency and abandonment being steep and unforgiving. We have compressed our waiting tolerance dramatically in one or two generations. What this means for the texture of human patience more broadly, I genuinely don't know. I suspect it means something, though I'm wary of the easy narrative that we are all becoming worse at patience in general. People still do very patient things. People still sit with the dying and stare at the sea and cultivate gardens that won't bloom for years. The impatience might be domain-specific, confined to the digital, which our brains have learned to treat by different rules.

The buffering circle is its own aesthetic object. It almost always involves rotation. Something turns, indicating process without indicating duration. Unlike the progress bar, which is an honest representation of incompleteness — here is what exists, here is what remains — the spinning circle tells you only that something is happening, not what or for how long. It is a symbol of pure process untethered from time. Sometimes it lies. Sometimes it spins while nothing is happening at all, while some process has silently crashed, and the spin continues as a kind of ghost, a habit the interface maintains after the substance has left.

---

Let me try to bring this somewhere, not to a conclusion exactly, because I don't think waiting resolves, but toward something.

I think we underestimate how much of life is waiting and therefore how much our experience of life depends on what we do with the texture of anticipation. Not what we do in the sense of productivity — I'm not interested in tips for making waiting "useful" — but what we do in the sense of what we allow ourselves to feel and notice.

The parking lot at eleven years old. The hold music becoming weirdly familiar. The friend's arrival erasing the waiting that preceded it. The pilgrim's feet on the road. These are all different facets of the same gem, which is: time before is still time, and it is happening to you, and it matters what you make of it even when you can't make anything of it.

There is a kind of dignity available in waiting that I think requires surrender to the waiting itself rather than constant combat against it. This isn't the same as enjoying it. The waiting room is not enjoyable. The parking lot at forty is not what the parking lot at eleven was. But there is a difference between waiting that is fighting itself, that is always pushing against its own duration, that has declared itself defeated before it ends — and waiting that simply is, that takes its place in the sequence of things without apology.

I don't achieve this. I pull out my phone. I manufacture things to do. I turn waiting into a kind of productivity theater even when the theater is embarrassing and fools no one including me. But I know that the other thing is possible because I remember it, from childhood, from occasional lapses into presence that I didn't plan.

Waiting is the default state of a lot of life. We are waiting for things to develop, for feelings to clarify, for other people to make their decisions, for the future to reveal its shape, for ourselves to become more or less who we are trying to be. Almost none of this resolves quickly or cleanly. Most of it involves a long and unannounced middle period of genuine unknowing.

What I want, and what I suspect most people want without being able to name it precisely, is to be able to wait without dissolving. To sit in the granular time, the textured time, without needing it to be something else immediately. To be in the waiting room of life and not pretend the room doesn't exist, not escape it through the phone or the narration or the performance of patience, but actually sit in those chairs — too close together, upholstered in that nameless color — and be there, fully, just waiting.

The name will be called. The train will come. Or it won't, and that will be its own news.

In the meantime, there is this.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
